The **Tokyo Metropolitan Government** (東京都庁, *Tōkyōto-chō*) is the government of the [Tokyo Metropolis](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo"). One of the 47 [prefectures](/wiki/Prefectures_of_Japan "Prefectures of Japan") of [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), the government consists of a popularly elected governor and assembly. The [headquarters building](/wiki/Tokyo_Metropolitan_Government_Building "Tokyo Metropolitan Government Building") is located in the ward of [Shinjuku](/wiki/Shinjuku,_Tokyo "Shinjuku, Tokyo"). The metropolitan government administers the [special wards](/wiki/Special_wards_of_Tokyo "Special wards of Tokyo"), cities, towns and villages that constitute part of the Tokyo Metropolis. With a population closing in on 14 million living within its boundaries, and many more commuting from neighbouring prefectures, the metropolitan government wields significant political power within Japan.

## Structure of Tokyo Metropolis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokyo_Metropolitan_Government&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Structure of Tokyo Metropolis")]

Main article: [Tokyo § Administration](/wiki/Tokyo#Administration "Tokyo")

Under [Japanese law](/wiki/Law_of_Japan "Law of Japan"), Tokyo is designated as a *to* ([都](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%83%BD "wiktionary:都")), translated as *[metropolis](/wiki/Metropolis "Metropolis")*.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Within Tokyo Metropolis lie dozens of smaller entities, including [twenty-three special wards](/wiki/Special_wards_of_Tokyo "Special wards of Tokyo") (特別[区](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%8C%BA "wiktionary:区") -ku) which until 1943 made up [Tokyo City](/wiki/Tokyo_City "Tokyo City") but which now have individual local governments, each with a leader and a council. In addition to these 23 local governments, Tokyo also encompasses 26 cities ([市](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%B8%82 "wiktionary:市") -shi), five towns ([町](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E7%94%BA "wiktionary:町") -chō or machi), and eight villages ([村](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%9D%91 "wiktionary:村") -son or -mura), each of which has a local government. These other municipalities are located in the [western part](/wiki/Western_Tokyo "Western Tokyo") of the prefecture, as well as the [outlying island](/wiki/Tokyo_Islands "Tokyo Islands") chains of [Izu](/wiki/Izu_Islands "Izu Islands") and [Ogasawara](/wiki/Ogasawara_Islands "Ogasawara Islands").

## Tokyo Metropolitan Assembly

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokyo_Metropolitan_Government&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Tokyo Metropolitan Assembly")]

Main article: [Tokyo Metropolitan Assembly](/wiki/Tokyo_Metropolitan_Assembly "Tokyo Metropolitan Assembly")

The Metropolitan Assembly is the legislative organ of the whole [prefecture of Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo"). It consists of 127 members elected each four years. Regular sessions are held four times each year, in February, June, September and December. These sessions typically last for 30 days. Between these are plenary sessions where discussions on bills are held.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Governor

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokyo_Metropolitan_Government&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Governor")]

Main article: [Governor of Tokyo](/wiki/Governor_of_Tokyo "Governor of Tokyo")

As in other [prefectures of Japan](/wiki/Prefectures_of_Japan "Prefectures of Japan"), the people of Tokyo directly elect the governor to four-year terms of office. There is no limit to the number of terms a person may serve. Unlike collegiate cabinet systems, where the decisions are made unanimously, the Governor has the authority to make policy decisions and enforce policy. As the chief executive of Tokyo, ruling an area encompassing 13 million inhabitants and a GDP comparable in size to some countries, they hold the greatest influence among Japan's governors. In contrast to other prefectures, the governor of Tokyo has a relatively important role given the size of Tokyo's budget (13 trillion yen as of 2014, which is roughly equivalent to the government budget of [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden"))[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]. The Tokyo metropolitan government is also granted relative freedom in how it allocates the budget, as it is not subject to national government subsidies which other prefectures receive. The responsibility for approving the metropolitan budget lies with the governor and the assembly. The assembly may vote for no confidence in the governor and the governor may order the assembly to be dissolved.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

[Karasumaru Mitsue](/w/index.php?title=Karasumaru_Mitsue&action=edit&redlink=1 "Karasumaru Mitsue (page does not exist)") served as the first prefectural governor of Edo Prefecture in 1868. Several months later, the prefecture was renamed to *Tokyo* and Karasumaru's tenure continued.

## Political parties and elections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokyo_Metropolitan_Government&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Political parties and elections")]

Tokyo's population consists largely of [swing voters](/wiki/Swing_voter "Swing voter") who are not loyal to any one political party. Tokyoites tend to vote for independent candidates with name recognition or in response to [hot-button issues](/wiki/Hot-button_issue "Hot-button issue"), and have been less susceptible to [pork-barrel spending](/wiki/Pork_barrel "Pork barrel") and other "machine" style politics than voters elsewhere in Japan.[[4]](#cite_note-jt0110-4)

With the early elections for the Metropolitan Assembly in 1965 due to a corruption scandal, Tokyo became the first prefecture not to hold its assembly elections during the unified local elections (*tōitsu chihō senkyo*), which typically take place in prefectures and municipalities throughout the country every four years. By [2011](/wiki/2011_Japanese_unified_local_elections "2011 Japanese unified local elections"), it was one of six prefectures not to do so, the others being [Iwate](/wiki/Iwate_Prefecture "Iwate Prefecture"), [Miyagi](/wiki/Miyagi_Prefecture "Miyagi Prefecture"), [Fukushima](/wiki/Fukushima_Prefecture "Fukushima Prefecture"), [Ibaraki](/wiki/Ibaraki_Prefecture "Ibaraki Prefecture") and [Okinawa](/wiki/Okinawa_Prefecture "Okinawa Prefecture"). Following Shintarō Ishihara's resignation in October 2012, Tokyo held an early gubernatorial election in December 2012 and completely left the unified election cycle.

The four largest established national political parties of the past decade (Liberal Democrats, Democrats, Kōmeitō, Communists) are represented in the Tokyo Assembly. The [Social Democratic Party](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_(Japan) "Social Democratic Party (Japan)"), formerly the Japanese Socialist Party, which had been the second major party for much of the postwar era, lost its one remaining seat in the [2001 election](/wiki/2001_Tokyo_prefectural_election "2001 Tokyo prefectural election").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

### Recent elections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tokyo_Metropolitan_Government&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Recent elections")]

Governor [Naoki Inose](/wiki/Naoki_Inose "Naoki Inose"), endorsed by the [Liberal Democratic Party](/wiki/Liberal_Democratic_Party_(Japan) "Liberal Democratic Party (Japan)"), [Kōmeitō](/wiki/K%C5%8Dmeit%C5%8D "Kōmeitō") and [Japan Restoration Party](/wiki/Japan_Restoration_Party "Japan Restoration Party"), won roughly two-thirds of the vote in the [2012 Tokyo gubernatorial election](/wiki/2012_Tokyo_gubernatorial_election "2012 Tokyo gubernatorial election"). Inose resigned in December 2013 and his successor [Yoichi Masuzoe](/wiki/Yoichi_Masuzoe "Yoichi Masuzoe") was elected in the [2014 Tokyo gubernatorial election](/wiki/2014_Tokyo_gubernatorial_election "2014 Tokyo gubernatorial election"). Masuzoe resigned in June 2016 and a new election was held on 31 July 2016. [Yuriko Koike](/wiki/Yuriko_Koike "Yuriko Koike"), former LDP defense minister but running as an independent, was elected with 44,49% of the popular vote.

The last assembly election was held on July 27. The new party of the governor Yuriko Koike
([Tomin First no Kai](/wiki/Tomin_First_no_Kai "Tomin First no Kai")) won 49 seats with 33.68% of the vote. The LDP obtained 23 seats with 22.53% of the vote. The [New Komeito Party](/wiki/New_Komeito_Party "New Komeito Party"), allied with Gov. Koike also obtained 23 seats, with 13.13% of the vote.

The previous election was held in June 2013. The LDP won 36% of the vote and 59 of 127 seats in the [2013 Tokyo prefectural election](/wiki/2013_Tokyo_prefectural_election "2013 Tokyo prefectural election"). In the previous [election of 2009](/wiki/2009_Tokyo_prefectural_election "2009 Tokyo prefectural election"), the Democratic Party had managed to become strongest party after forty years of LDP dominance. In 2012, the DPJ was reduced to fourth party with 15 seats (15.2% of the vote) as the Kōmeitō won 23 seats (14.1% of the vote) and Communists 17 seats (13.6% of the vote).
